flashcards.back: Injective functions map distinct domain elements to distinct codomain elements (one-to-one).
A function where every codomain element has at least one pre-image in the domain (onto).
Combines injective and surjective properties (both one-to-one and onto).
Applying function g first, then f to the result: (f∘g)(x) = f(g(x))
Only bijective functions have true inverses that satisfy f(f⁻¹(y)) = y.
Function that always returns the same value regardless of input.
Function that returns its input unchanged: f(x) = x
Returns 1 if input belongs to a specific set, 0 otherwise.
Floor rounds down, ceiling rounds up to nearest integer.

flashcards.front: What's a hash function's key property?
flashcards.back: Deterministic mapping from large input space to fixed-size output.
Order matters: f∘(g∘h) ≠ (f∘g)∘h in general cases.

flashcards.front: Why are bijections crucial in cryptography?
flashcards.back: Ensure unique, reversible mappings for encryption/decryption.
Function undefined for some domain elements.
Bijective function from a set to itself.
If |A| > |B|, no injective function exists from A→B.
